title: Kafka & Messaging Systems
description: Apache Kafka and messaging systems in general: the log model, partitions, consumer groups, delivery semantics, replication and choosing a broker.
questions: What is Apache Kafka, and how does its distributed-log model differ from a traditional message broker like RabbitMQ where messages are removed once consumed? What new use cases does keeping the log open up?

Explain topics, partitions and offsets. What ordering guarantee does Kafka actually give, and how does the message key determine which partition a record lands on — with what consequence for ordering?

What is a consumer group? Explain how partitions are assigned to consumers in a group, and how the same topic can serve both a work-queue pattern (competing consumers) and a pub/sub pattern (multiple groups).

Compare the delivery semantics in messaging systems: at-most-once, at-least-once and exactly-once. What do the producer's `acks` setting, the idempotent producer and Kafka transactions each contribute?
How does consumer offset management work? Compare auto-commit with manual commit (before vs after processing), and explain how each choice maps to message loss vs duplicate processing after a crash.
Explain Kafka replication: partition leaders and followers, the in-sync replica (ISR) set, and how `acks=all` together with `min.insync.replicas` prevents data loss when a broker fails.
Compare time/size-based retention with log compaction. How does a compacted topic behave, and when would you use one (e.g. changelog, event sourcing, restoring state)?

What goes wrong operationally with partitions? Discuss choosing a partition count (and why increasing it later breaks key ordering), hot partitions caused by skewed keys, and what a consumer-group rebalance does to processing.

What is consumer lag, why does it matter, and how do you deal with a consumer group that can't keep up? Discuss scaling limits (consumers vs partitions), batch tuning and backpressure in log-based vs push-based brokers.

You need asynchronous communication between services: when would you pick Kafka, when a queue broker like RabbitMQ, and when neither (just synchronous calls)? Compare them on throughput, ordering, routing flexibility, replay and operational cost.
